personality_voice.book_baseline: Winky is a deeply loyal, tradition-bound house-elf defined by her devotion to the Crouch family, her shame at being freed, her internalized belief in elf subservience, and her emotional volatility. She speaks in fragmented, self-referential third-person squeaks, oscillates between terror, fury, and grief, and is fiercely protective of her master's secrets even while visibly falling apart. Her voice is distinctive: she defends Crouch even as he destroys her, scolds Dobby for his freedom, and collapses into drunken despair at Hogwarts.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: Winky does not appear in any film scene. The film corpus is entirely empty.
personality_voice.difference: The character is completely absent from the films. No trait, line, behavior, or moment is preserved because none is present.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: A score of 0 is the only possible score. There is no film portrayal to evaluate.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Winky serves multiple structural functions: she is the inciting figure at the World Cup whose presence under the Dark Mark drives the investigation; she is the elf who spent months persuading Crouch Sr. to bring Barty Jr. to the Cup, directly enabling Voldemort's return; she physically restrains Barty Jr. in the forest, preventing a worse outcome; she is a living witness to the Crouch family secret; and her drunken state at Hogwarts provides Harry with partial clues about Crouch Sr.'s absence. She is not decorative - she initiates, restrains, conceals, and inadvertently reveals.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Winky does not appear in any film. Her narrative functions are either cut entirely or, in the case of the Barty Crouch Jr. subplot, handled through different means.
narrative_role_agency.difference: All agency is absent. The entire narrative role is eliminated.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Score of 0. No agency can be preserved when the character does not exist in the films.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Winky's core motivation is loyalty to the Crouch family as an identity, not merely a duty. She is internally conflicted: she knows things that could harm her master, she is complicit in concealing Barty Jr., and she is simultaneously a victim of Crouch Sr.'s cruelty and his most devoted defender. Her refusal to accept freedom, her shame, and her descent into butterbeer dependency all stem from a self-concept entirely built around servitude. Her conflict is between what she knows (Crouch is cruel, she did nothing wrong) and what she believes (she is a bad elf who deserved to be freed as punishment).
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: All motivations and internal conflict are absent.

character_arc.book_baseline: Winky begins as a loyal, functioning house-elf with a clear sense of purpose and identity, is publicly humiliated and freed against her will, and ends in a state of psychological collapse - drunk, filthy, and unable to reorient her identity around anything other than a master who discarded her. It is a tragic arc of institutional trauma: she cannot accept freedom because her entire self-worth was constructed around servitude. The arc ends without resolution; she is still drinking in book 5.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships: (1) Crouch Sr. - a relationship of total devotion meeting total indifference and cruelty; she defends him even after he destroys her. (2) Barty Jr. - she nursed him, advocated for him, and physically protected him; she is devastated by his confession. (3) Dobby - a foil relationship showing two possible responses to freedom; their dynamic at Hogwarts is the primary vehicle for exploring elf psychology.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Winky is one of the richest minor characters in the series in terms of thematic function. She embodies the horror of internalized oppression more completely than any other elf - she is not happy in servitude like the Hogwarts elves claim to be, she is visibly broken by it, yet she still defends the system. She holds plot-critical secrets, she is complicit in concealment, she is a victim who enables her own victimizer, and her drunken collapse is both comic and genuinely tragic. She also provides the key clue about Bagman and keeps the Crouch family secret under duress.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: All complexity, nuance, thematic function, and lost material are entirely absent from the films.

lost_or_transferred_material: Winky's entire presence at the Quidditch World Cup Top Box
Winky being found stunned beneath the Dark Mark with Harry's wand
Winky's public dismissal by Crouch Sr. and her terror at receiving clothes
Winky's months-long campaign to persuade Crouch Sr. to bring Barty Jr. to the World Cup - the act that enabled Voldemort's return
Winky physically restraining Barty Jr. in the forest to prevent him attacking Death Eaters
Winky's arrival at Hogwarts and her psychological collapse into butterbeer dependency
Winky as a foil to Dobby illustrating two responses to forced freedom
Winky's role as keeper of the Crouch family secret (Barty Jr. alive)
Winky's clue about Bagman being a 'bad wizard'
Winky's reaction during Barty Jr.'s Veritaserum confession - her horror, her attempts to silence him, her wails
Winky's thematic function as the primary illustration of internalized oppression in the elf subplot
Winky being sent to Moody's office by Dumbledore after the revelation scene
Winky still drinking in book 5, referenced by Dobby, with the Room of Requirement used to hide her
score_caps_applied: No caps needed - raw dimension scores already total 0 due to complete absence from films
comparative.key_observations: Winky does not appear in any Harry Potter film. The film corpus is empty. Every score is 0 not as a judgment of adaptation quality but as a statement of fact: there is no film portrayal to evaluate. The confidence is High precisely because the absence is total and unambiguous. Winky's removal from the films eliminates a character who was structurally important to the Barty Crouch Jr. plot, thematically central to the house-elf subplot, and one of the series' most complete illustrations of internalized oppression. Her absence required the films to handle the Crouch family secret through other means and left the house-elf theme almost entirely to Dobby.
